coredata.dc:title: Intercalation of novel Ocimum Sanctum leaves derived carbon dots between g-C3N4/CoFe2O4 Z-scheme heterojunction system for boosting the radicals’ generation in photo-Fenton degradation and synchronized electrochemical sensing of sulfamethoxazole antibiotic

coredata.dc:description: Deeming about incessant consumption and disposal of pharmaceutical antibiotics in natural water bodies, this study demonstrates the synthesis of a novel Tulsi leaves (Ocimum Sanctum) derived carbon dots modified Z-scheme g-C3N4/CoFe2O4 heterojunction system. Characterization techniques namely XRD, FT-IR, XPS, FE-SEM, HR-TEM and EDX supported composite formation. Fabricated catalysts presented excellent photocatalytic performance towards removal of sulfamethoxazole (SMX). UV–vis DRS, PL and EIS findings suggested the augmented visible light absorption capability, suppression of electron-hole pairs recombination and effective separation of charge carriers which were accountable for degradation process. Probable mechanistic pathway for SMX removal was photo-Fenton assisted with Z-scheme separated electron-hole pairs via activation of H2O2, where hydroxyl radicals (•OH) were main reactive species. Additionally, the prepared material was employed as electrochemical sensor for individual as well as simultaneous detection of SMX and trimethoprim with quite impressive detection limits of 0.042 µM and 0.047 µM, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of CN, CoFe, CNCoFe-20, CDs, 0.25-CDCNCoFe, 0.5-CDCNCoFe, 0.75-CDCNCoFe and 1-CDCNCoFe.
XPS spectra of CN, CoFe, CNCoFe-20 and 0.5-CDCNCoFe: (a) full survey scan; deconvulated spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) Co 2p, (e) Fe 2p and (f) O 1 s.
FE-SEM micrographs at (a) 400 nm, (b) 500 nm and (c) 1 µm resolution; (d-i) elemental mapping of 0.5-CDCNCoFe heterostructure.
TEM images of (a) CN, (b) CoFe2O4, (c) CDs; (d, e) low resolution TEM images, (f) high resolution TEM image, (g) SAED pattern and (h) EDX spectra of 0.5-CDCNCoFe heterostructure.
Room temperature magnetic hysteresis loops of CoFe, CNCoFe-20, 0.25-CDCNCoFe, 0.5-CDCNCoFe, 0.75-CDCNCoFe and 1-CDCNCoFe (Inset demonstrating the corresponding magnified view of hysteresis loops).

(a) UV–vis DRS spectra of CN, CoFe, CNCoFe-20, 0.25-CDCNCoFe, 0.5-CDCNCoFe, 0.75-CDCNCoFe and 1-CDCNCoFe; (b, c) Tauc plots of CN and CoFe; (d) PL spectra of CN, CoFe, CNCoFe-20, 0.25-CDCNCoFe, 0.5-CDCNCoFe, 0.75-CDCNCoFe and 1-CDCNCoFe.

(a) Comparative CVs of catalysts modified GCE obtained at the scan rate of 100 mVs−1 in 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution containing 0.1 M KCl, (b) variation of peak current (Ip) of 0.5-CDCNCoFe/GCE with square root of scan rate (v1/2), (c) Nyquist EIS plots of all the synthesized materials and (d) corresponding Randles equivalent circuit of 0.5-CDCNCoFe/GCE.

(a) % degradation v/s time plots and (b) pseudo first order rate kinetics for the removal of SMX using CN, CoFe, CNCoFe-20, 0.25-CDCNCoFe, 0.5-CDCNCoFe, 0.75-CDCNCoFe and 1-CDCNCoFe, where pH = 2.5, [catalyst] = 0.5 g/L and [H2O2] = 8.8 mM.
TOC removal % of SMX by all the fabricated materials.
Effect of (a) different anionic species and (b) HA concentration on % degradation of SMX using 0.5-CDCNCoFe heterostructure.
Proposed illustration of Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism occurring in CDCNCoFe heterostructure.

SWVs of 0.5-CDCNCoFe/GCE recorded in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7) consisting of varying concentration of (a) SMX from 0.05 µM to 1 µM (keeping [TMP] = 0.1 µM), (b) TMP from 0.05 µM to 1 µM (keeping [SMX] = 0.35 µM) and (c, d) their corresponding calibration curves at the scan rate of 100 mVs−1.

SWVs of 0.5-CDCNCoFe/GCE recorded in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7) consisting of simultaneously varying concentration of SMX and TMP from 0.05 µM to 1 µM and (b) their corresponding calibration curves at the scan rate of 100 mVs−1.
Different magnetic parameters of the fabricated materials.
Energy band gap values and corresponding conduction and valence band edge potentials of CN and CoFe.
Degradation % alongwith the respective rate constant values for photo-oxidation of SMX by all the fabricated materials.
Comparison of various photocatalysts for detection of SMX.
Comparison of various modified electrodes for detection of SMX and TMP.
Simultaneous determination of SMX and TMP in real water samples.

head.ce:title: Intercalation of novel Ocimum Sanctum leaves derived carbon dots between g-C3N4/CoFe2O4 Z-scheme heterojunction system for boosting the radicals’ generation in photo-Fenton degradation and synchronized electrochemical sensing of sulfamethoxazole antibiotic

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Deeming about incessant consumption and disposal of pharmaceutical antibiotics in natural water bodies, this study demonstrates the synthesis of a novel Tulsi leaves (Ocimum Sanctum) derived carbon dots modified Z-scheme g-C3N4/CoFe2O4 heterojunction system. Characterization techniques namely XRD, FT-IR, XPS, FE-SEM, HR-TEM and EDX supported composite formation. Fabricated catalysts presented excellent photocatalytic performance towards removal of sulfamethoxazole (SMX). UV–vis DRS, PL and EIS findings suggested the augmented visible light absorption capability, suppression of electron-hole pairs recombination and effective separation of charge carriers which were accountable for degradation process. Probable mechanistic pathway for SMX removal was photo-Fenton assisted with Z-scheme separated electron-hole pairs via activation of H2O2, where hydroxyl radicals (•OH) were main reactive species. Additionally, the prepared material was employed as electrochemical sensor for individual as well as simultaneous detection of SMX and trimethoprim with quite impressive detection limits of 0.042 µM and 0.047 µM, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A novel Tulsi leaves (Ocimum Sanctum) derived carbon dots modified Z-scheme g-C3N4/CoFe2O4 heterojunction system was fabricated and employed for dual purpose of photo-Fenton assisted Z-scheme based photocatalytic degradation of sulfamethoxazole (SMX) and simultaneous electrochemical detection of SMX and trimethoprim (TMP).

ce:section.ce:para: The past decade has evidenced the continual expansion in consumption of antibiotics and their widespread discharge into natural water resources which consequently poses potential threat to eco-system, aquatic life as well as human health (Ritter et al., 2002; Guo et al., 2021). Sulfamethoxazole (SMX), a sulfonamide antibiotic, is widely employed as therapeutic agent for curing bacterial infections in humans as well as animals (Guo et al., 2020). Because of the prevalent usage of SMX, it is being detected in surface water, potable water and municipal effluent discharge. Though the concentration of SMX found in different water sources is very low (ngL−1 to µgL−1); however, due to its high toxicity, resistance to degradation and microorganisms, International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) and World Health Organization (WHO) has claimed it as an impending pollutant (Reddy et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2022; Dhiman et al., 2021). In this regard, researchers have been unceasingly putting efforts upon developing new and satisfactory methods for detection and removal of SMX residues from contaminated water (Zhang et al., 2023a; Xue et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2020).

Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs), basically semiconductor based photocatalytic oxidation, have been considered as highly efficient, safe, clean and promising technology for decomposition of toxins from wastewater (Lee et al., 2020). From few years, numerous research cohorts have been exploiting a 2D metal free graphitic-Carbon Nitride (CN) as a proficient candidate for visible light driven photocatalysis, owing to its easy preparation, high stability, tunable properties and appropriate band gap energy (∼ 2.7 eV). However, the use of single component CN photocatalyst suffers from high photoinduced charge carrier recombination alongwith little visible light harnessing capability (Xiao et al., 2020; Ong et al., 2016). In this quest, combination of different semiconductors in such a way so as to configure Z-scheme heterojunction encompassing higher charge separability, good redox potential and improved visible light harvesting competency can prove to be a good discretion. Hitherto, various CN based Z-scheme heterojunction systems have been designed and successfully implemented in removing contaminants from aquatic system (Liu et al., 2023; Sepehrmansourie et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2023b; Yang et al., 2022; Yi et al., 2022).

Besides photocatalytic oxidation, heterogeneous photo-Fenton oxidation also serve as an assuring technology for the degradation of noxious pollutants from wastewater by utilizing the H2O2 (as oxidizing agent) and generating hydroxyl radicals (•OH) for the oxidation of contaminant molecules (Xie et al., 2024; Yuan et al., 2023; Zhou et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2021a; Li et al., 2019, 2023a; Lama et al., 2022). Typically, Fe based oxides display excellent photo-Fenton performance, where Cobalt Ferrite (CoFe) has been extensively utilized as photo-Fenton catalyst owing to its easy preparation, chemical stability and good catalytic performance (Wang et al., 2023). Yet, low carrier mobility and high charge recombination rate hinder its practical activity. To address these challenges, several studies demonstrate the clubbing of photo-Fenton system with Z-scheme system which exhibit outstanding photocatalytic oxidation performance due to the higher oxidation proficiency and effective separation of charge carriers at heterojunction interface (Ye et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021b; Hu et al., 2023). Still, to foster the efficient charge transfer route at the heterojunction interface is problematic to attain.

Recently, a new class of carbon nanomaterial, Carbon Dots (CDs), comprising of hybrid sp2/sp3 carbon atoms, are significantly being employed to modify Z-scheme heterostructures owing to their unique optical properties, chemical stability, high aqueous solubility and electron transfer properties (Zhao et al., 2022). Different carbon sources can be utilized for preparation of CDs, out of which, predominantly, the synthetic methodology utilizing green source for CDs formation comes in the upfront with the view to convert waste to value added products. Additionally, CDs display magnificent absorption in the whole solar spectrum and thus possess up-converted photoluminescence (PL) characteristics. Therefore, various studies reporting the modification of semiconductor with CDs are present in literature which clearly allude that CDs not only help in effective separation of charge carriers, but also broader the visible light absorption range, thereby enhancing the quantum efficiency and degradation ability of semiconductors (Li et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2023).

Besides eradication of contaminants from wastewater, there has been increasing demand of their identification, which has led to the growth in electrochemical sensing technique because of its easy handling, fast response and better selectivity (Yeh et al., 2022). Semiconductor based heterojunction systems exhibit high charge transfer rate and charge carriers mobility that endures the system with good electrochemical response (Yue et al., 2020; Wei et al., 2021).

With these motivations, herein a novel Tulsi leaves (Ocimum Sanctum) derived CDs modified CN/CoFe heterojunction system (CDCNCoFe) was designed and successfully synthesized via sonication-calcination route. Different structural, morphological and optical characterizations ascertained the successful formation of fabricated materials. Owing to the synergistic impact of photo-Fenton mechanism in combination with Z-scheme charge transfer route, the fabricated heterostructures were employed for the photooxidative removal of SMX by the activation of H2O2. Furthermore, due to the high transference of charge carriers and suitable electrochemical current response, the manufactured heterostructure was utilized in the simultaneous electrochemical detection of antibiotics, SMX and trimethoprim (TMP).

All the chemicals utilized in this work were of analytical grade and used as such without further purification. Urea (99 %) was attained from Finar limited. Cobaltous nitrate (Co(NO3)3.6 H2O, 99.5 %), ferric nitrate (Fe(NO3)3.9 H2O, 98 %), hydrogen peroxide (30 % w/v), p-benzoquinone (98 %), ammonium oxalate (99 %), isopropyl alcohol (99.5 %), sulfamethoxazole (98 %) and trimethoprim (98 %) were acquired from Central Drug House (CDH) Pvt. Ltd. Sodium dihydrogen orthophosphate monohydrate (NaH2PO4·H2O, 99 %), Disodium hydrogen orthophosphate anhydrous (Na2HPO4, 98 %), potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6)], 98 %), potassium ferrocyanide (K4[Fe(CN)6)], 98 %) and potassium chloride (KCl, 99.5 %) were procured from TCI chemicals. All the solutions for photocatalytic studies were prepared in distilled water and deionized water was utilized for preparing solutions for electrochemical measurements.

Different characterization tools were employed to affirm the successful formation of fabricated materials. To predict the crystal phases, Powder X-ray Diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out on panalytical’s X’Pert pro diffractometer with scan angle (2Ө) of 10⸰ to 100⸰. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra was recorded on Nicolet iS50 FTIR instrument with the scan range of 4000–400 cm−1. To determine the chemical composition, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was employed using Nexsa Base (Thermofisher Scientific). To have a deep insight into morphology, Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM, Hitachi-SU8010) and High Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Tecnai (G2F20)) were exploited. To look into the elemental composition, Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy adjacent to HR-TEM system was utilized. The magnetic properties of fabricated heterostructures was measured on Microsense EZ7 Vibrating Sample Magnetometer (VSM). The optical properties of all prepared materials were assessed through Ultraviolet-visible Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS) having the scan range of 200 nm to 800 nm. To delve into charge transfer occurring in fabricated materials, Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on Agilent Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (G9800A). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurements were performed to determine the surface area of prepared materials using Belsorp max BET analyser. All the electrochemical experiments were performed on Metrohm Autolab/PGSTAT204 electrochemical analyser. The photocatalytic degradation experiments were confirmed via gradual reduction in absorption intensity of SMX in UV–vis spectrophotometer (JASCO model V-750). The total organic content (TOC) was scrutinized via Shimadzu TOC-L analyzer.

CN was obtained by direct pyrolysis of urea in air as described in our previous report (Garg et al., 2022). Cobalt ferrite (CoFe) was prepared by conventional sol-gel methodology (Garg et al., 2023a). In the typical procedure, required amounts of metal nitrates were dissolved in minimum amount of distilled water. To this solution, appropriate amount of citric acid solution (as chelating agent) was added, followed by the slow addition of ethylene glycol (as gelating agent). The as-formed solution was kept at magnetic stirrer with temperature maintained at 80–90 οC, which afterwards led to the gel formation. Upon continuous heating, gel get converted to dried powder. The collected powder was kept for annealing at 400 οC for 2 h to procure the required CoFe nanoparticles.

A simple sonication-calcination strategy was employed for the fabrication of CN/CoFe heterostructures. Herein, different quantities of CN and CoFe were dispersed in 60 mL of ethanol: water solution (1:1) via ultrasonication for 30 min. Afterwards, the solution was kept in oil bath placed on magnetic stirrer maintained at 70–80 οC for the complete evaporation of solvent system. Finally, the acquired powder sample was placed in muffle furnace at 400 ⸰C for 2 h with ramp rate of 5 οCmin−1. The procured CN/CoFe heterostructures were designated as CNCoFe-x, where x is the weight ratios of CoFe to CN with x = 10 %, 20 %, 30 % and 40 % of CoFe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon dots from Tulsi leaves (Ocimum Sanctum)
ce:section.ce:para: Green source, namely Krishna Tulsi leaves (Ocimum Sanctum) was employed for the preparation of CDs via facile hydrothermal methodology (Kaur et al., 2023a). Typically, 15 g of freshly obtained Tulsi leaves was macerated in 120 mL of distilled water by using household mixer. Afterwards, the solution was transferred to 120 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and was placed in hot-air oven maintained at 180 οC for 6 h to undergo the hydrothermal treatment. Followed by cooling at room temperature, the as-obtained residue were filtered to procure a yellow-brown solution, which was further dialyzed against distilled water for 18 h to remove any unwanted ions. The obtained solution was freeze dried to procure brown solid particles of CDs.

Varied amounts of CDs and CNCoFe-20 were disseminated in 60 mL ethanol: water (1:1) solution using ultra-sonicator for 1 h. Subsequently, the solution was placed in oil bath maintained at constant temperature of 70–80 οC till the solvents get completely removed. The as-formed powder was calcined at 300 ⸰C for 2 h with ramp rate of 5 οCmin−1. The procured CDCNCoFe heterostructures were named as y-CDCNCoFe, where y is the weight ratios of CD to CNCoFe-20 with x = 0.25 %, 0.5 %, 0.75 % and 1 % of CDs.

The photocatalytic performance of as-prepared materials was evaluated via degradation of SMX (50 mg/L) under visible light irradiation under optimized conditions. Typically, 100 mL of SMX solution was taken and its pH was adjusted to 2.5 by dilute H2SO4. 50 mg of photocatalyst was dispersed in this solution and kept on magnetic stirrer under dark conditions for 30 min so as to acquire adsorption-desorption equilibria. Subsequently, required concentration of H2O2 was added to the reaction mixture and straightaway subjected to visible light irradiation. Afterwards, 3 mL of aliquots were extracted from the reaction mixture at fixed interval of time and the residual concentration of pollutant was assessed through UV–vis spectrophotometer.

Prior to the electrochemical experiments, Glassy Carbon Electrode (GCE) was polished with alumina slurry in figure-8 motion and further washed with absolute ethanol and deionized water to remove any impurities. The rinsed GCE was subsequently kept for drying at room temperature. For the electrochemical measurements, 0.5-CDCNCoFe solution with concentration of 1 mg/mL was prepared. Afterwards, for every electrochemical run, pre-treated GCE was modified by drop-casting 2 µL of the above prepared suspension followed by drying at room temperature. Square wave voltammetry (SWV) was employed for the determination of SMX and TMP in 0.1 M phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.0) over the potential range from +0.7 V to +1.5 V at the scan rate of 100 mVs−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase structure and chemical composition analysis: XRD, FT-IR and XPS
ce:section.ce:para: To analyse the structural aspects and crystallinity of synthesized samples, XRD measurements were carried out. XRD diffractograms of pristine CN, CoFe, CDs, binary CNCoFe-20 and CDs modified CNCoFe-20 heterostructures are epitomized in Fig. 1. The two distinctive peaks occurring at 2Ө values of 13.2 ᵒ and 27.8 ᵒ in the spectra of CN corresponded to (100) and (002) planes of tri-s-triazine units and interplanar stacked structure of conjugated aromatic rings of CN, respectively (JCPDS 87–1526) (Reddy et al., 2023). The XRD patterns of CoFe constituted of peaks around 30.7 ᵒ, 35.7 ᵒ, 36.2 ᵒ, 43.1 ᵒ, 54.6 ᵒ, 56.7 ᵒ and 63.1 ᵒ values were indexed to (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511) and (440) planes, respectively, which well-matched with standard JCPDS 01–1121 of cubic CoFe (Garg et al., 2023a). The existence of all the peaks with respect to pure CN and CoFe in the XRD spectra of binary CNCoFe-20 confirms the formation of heterostructure. A broad diffraction hump appearing at 10 ᵒ - 25 ᵒ in the diffraction array of CDs were the characteristic of graphite structure. Moreover, the sharp peaks at 28.6 ᵒ and 40.9 ᵒ paralleled to (220) facet (d = 0.318 nm) and (100) facet (d = 0.21 nm) of graphite like CDs. Consonant outcomes of Tulsi derived CDs have been presented by Renu et al. and Doshi et al (Kaur et al., 2023a; Doshi and Mungray, 2020). The simultaneous occurrence of the XRD peaks related to each individual component of CDs modified CNCoFe-20 heterostructure inveterate their successful fabrication.

To substantiate the presence of different functional groups as well as molecular structure of synthesized materials, FT-IR analysis was executed. FT-IR spectra of all the prepared samples are presented in Fig. S1. FT-IR spectra of pure CN constitutes of a sharp peak around 805 cm−1 which corresponded to out-of-plane bending modes of tri-s-triazine units. A broad peak appearing in the region of 1200–1600 cm−1 was attributed to CN stretching modes, C-N bending modes and heterocycles CN. Another broad band around 3100–3600 cm−1 matched to stretching vibrations of –NH2/NH group present at the sites of aromatic rings of CN (Wang et al., 2021b). The absorption peaks at 459 cm−1 and 599 cm−1 emerging in the spectra of CoFe were endorsed to Fe-O and Co-O stretching vibrations (Hafeez et al., 2019). Further, the FT-IR spectra of CDs constituted of sharp peaks at 1620 cm−1 (CO stretching vibrations of carbonyl group), 1381 cm−1 (O-H bending vibrations) and a broad dip between 3100 and 3600 cm−1 (N-H and O-H stretching vibrations of amine and hydroxyl groups) (Li et al., 2021; Kaur et al., 2023a). The presence of distinctive peaks analogous to every component in the synthesized CNCoFe-20 and CDs modified CNCoFe-20 confirms their successful incorporation.

To corroborate the existence of various specific bonds and chemical states of elements present in CN, CoFe, CNCoFe-20 and 0.5-CDCNCoFe heterostructure, XPS analysis was accomplished. Fig. 2. displays the XPS survey scan along with deconvulated spectra of the corresponding individual elements present in all the fabricated materials. XPS survey scan (Fig. 2. (a)) constituted of the peaks corresponding to signals of C, N, Co, Fe and O in the respective compounds confirming the presence of all these elements in synthesized materials. The high resolution XPS spectra of C 1 s CN can be deconvulated in two major peaks (Fig. 2. (b)) spotted at 284.6 eV and 288.1 eV which could be attributed to C-C bond and sp2 carbon atoms covalently attached to N-containing aromatic rings, respectively, asserting the formation of CN (Ye et al., 2018; Hu et al., 2023). Additionally, a minor peak appearing at 293.2 eV was assigned to the satellite features arising due to heterocycles ᴨ-excitations of CN. Upon the formation of binary and ternary composites, a slight shift in binding energy values for peaks related to C 1 s was observed which confirmed the construction of heterojunction. Besides, a small signal at 286.7 eV was due to the presence of CO/C-O bond that confirms the incorporation of CDs in 0.5-CDCNCoFe heterostructure (Kaur et al., 2023a; Zhu et al., 2023). The XPS spectra of N 1 s in CN (Fig. 2. (c)) encompasses three peak signals at 399.2 eV, 400.3 eV and 401.6 eV which were allocated to N-atom covalently bonded to sp2 C atoms (C-NC), terminal N-atom covalently attracted to C-atoms (N-(C)3) and terminal amino functional group attached to H-atom (C-N-H), respectively (Yi et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2019). The shifting in binding energy of C-NC bond was relative to binding energy of C-N-C bonds appearing in spectra of C 1 s, thus affirming the fabrication of heterostructures. The Co 2p spectra (Fig. 2. (d)) was fitted into two main peaks at binding energies of 780.8 eV and 794.6 eV which corresponded to Co+2 2p3/2 and Co+2 2p1/2; and the two satellite peaks were observed at 785.9 eV and 802.0 eV owing to Co+2 oxidation state (Hafeez et al., 2019). The two prominent peaks appearing at 711.9 eV and 724.3 eV in the deconvulated XPS spectra of Fe 2p (Fig. 2(e)) were attributable to Fe+2 2p3/2 and Fe+2 2p1/2; whereas the two weak signals at 718.2 eV and 724.8 eV were the satellite peaks of Fe+3 ions (Garg et al., 2023a). The alteration of signal appearing due to Co+2 2p1/2 and Fe+2 2p1/2 in Co 2p and Fe 2p spectra towards lower values in 0.5-CDCNCoFe heterostructure, further affirmed the successful incorporation of CoFe into ternary composite. The high-resolution XPS survey spectra of O 1 s composed of three peaks at binding energy values of 529.7 eV, 530.2 eV and 530.5 eV corresponding to Co/Fe-O bond of CoFe, CO and C-OH bonds of chemisorbed oxygen species (Wu et al., 2023; Xie et al., 2018). Thus, XPS analysis ascertain the fruitful fabrication of pristine, binary and ternary composites as well as existence of CN, CoFe and CDs in 0.5-CDCNCoFe heterostructure.

To examine the surface morphology and microstructure of prepared heterostructure, FE-SEM images of 0.5-CDCNCoFe were acquired, as displayed in Fig. 3. As inferred from the FE-SEM micrographs (Fig. 3. (a-c)), CN was perceived to be present in two-dimensional graphitic layered sheet like structure over which the spherical CoFe nanoparticles alongwith CDs were decorated. These outcomes reveal the close interfacial contact between all the components present in heterostructure. Further, the elemental mapping (Fig. 3(d-f)) divulges the distribution of C, N, Co, Fe and O in 0.5-CDCNCoFe matrix.

Besides, to attain deep discernment about the structure and lattice parameters, HR-TEM analysis was performed. Fig. 4. (a) reveals sheet like structure of pristine CN, Fig. 4. (b) predicts the spherical shaped magnetic CoFe nanoparticles and Fig. 4. (c) displays the well distributed spherical CDs. Fig. 4. (d, e) unveils the low resolution and high-resolution TEM images of 0.5-CDCNCoFe. Flaky sheet like layered structure of CN was shrouded with randomly distributed larger spherical particles of CoFe and smaller particles of CDs, uncovering the good distribution of CN, CoFe and CDs in the fabricated 0.5-CDCNCoFe matrix. These results discovered the good interfacial interaction of all the components in heterostructure, thus making the charge separation pathway easier. These results were also in concordance with FE-SEM outcomes. The lattice fringe spacing of 0.25 nm and 0.21 nm, appearing in high resolution TEM image (Fig. 4. (f)), corresponded to (311) and (002) crystal planes of CoFe and graphite structure of CDs, respectively. Additionally, SAED pattern (Fig. 4. (g)) constituted of concentric rings corresponding to the crystal planes which displays the crystalline nature of synthesized heterostructure. These results were in full agreement with XRD measurements, thereby suggesting the successful formation of heterostructure. Moreover, elemental analysis was carried out (Fig. 4. (h)) which marked the existence of only required elements in 0.5-CDCNCoFe, hence substantiating the purity of synthesized heterostructure.

The room temperature magnetic behaviour of the synthesized CoFe, CNCoFe-20 and y-CDCNCoFe heterostructures were scrutinized through VSM analysis. The hysteresis loops for samples were recorded by varying the magnetic field between −10,000 to +10,000 Oe and the results are displayed in Fig. 5. (inset showing the magnified view of hysteresis loop). The S-shaped magnetic hysteresis loops obtained for all the materials displayed the ferromagnetic character owing to the presence of CoFe in the fabricated materials (Singh et al., 2022). The magnetic saturation (Ms) value was discerned to be maximum for CoFe. The undoubted reduction in Ms value of CoFe upon the formation of heterostructures might be attributed to the introduction of non-magnetic CN and CDs to the system. It is noteworthy that although the Ms values underwent significant decline, however the synthesized CDCNCoFe heterostructure exhibited sufficient magnetism that facilitate its recoverability and recyclability using external magnets, thereby contriving the material with economical and environment-friendly benefits to treat wastewater in practical world. Table 1. embodies the analogous values of various magnetic parameters such as saturation magnetization (Ms), remanent magnetization (Mr), coercivity (Hc) and squareness ratio (S=Mr/Ms), as obtained from hysteresis loops.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical property investigation: UV–vis DRS and PL
ce:section.ce:para: To introspect the optical properties of synthesized materials, UV–vis DRS technique was employed. As depicted in Fig. 6. (a), pristine CN exhibited a typical absorption edge around 455 nm, while pure CoFe nanoparticles displayed good adsorption covering the entire visible range. Upon the introduction of CoFe to CN, the prepared CNCoFe-20 heterostructure exhibited slight red shift with expansive visible light absorption capacity. Interestingly, with the further incorporation of CDs to CNCoFe-20 composite, CDCNCoFe heterostructures presented higher absorption even more than CNCoFe-20 composite in the entire visible light range, reasoning for the boosted photocatalytic performance of CDs modified CNCoFe-20 composite.

Kubelka-Munk transformation was employed to calculate the band gap energies of all the synthesized materials, as given in Eq. (1) (Singh et al., 2022):
(1)
α h ν = A
( h ν −
E
g
)
1 / 2
Where α is absorption coefficient, h is Plank’s constant, ν is frequency of light in Hz, A is proportionality constant and Eg is the band gap energy value in eV. Tauc plots were plotted between (αhν)2 vs. hν plots (Fig. 6. (b, c) and Fig. S2.) and according to the linear portion of this curve, band gap energies of CN, CoFe, CNCoFe-20, 0.25-CDCNCoFe, 0.5-CDCNCoFe, 0.75-CDCNCoFe and 1-CDCNCoFe were appraised to be around 2.91 eV, 1.39 eV, 2.88 eV, 2.85 eV, 2.80 eV, 2.82 eV and 2.86 eV, respectively. The decline in band gap energy values upon the inclusion of CDs to CNCoFe-20 composite facilitates the effective separation of photogenerated charge carriers responsible for photocatalytic oxidation of target pollutant (Li et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019).

Furthermore, to get insight about the band structure of fabricated heterostructure, the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) edge potentials were evaluated by employing the below mentioned empirical formulas (Zhang et al., 2019; Garg et al., 2023a):
(2)

Where, ECB and EVB are the band potentials of corresponding CB and VB, Ⅹ is electronegativity of semiconductor, Ee is the energy of free electron on hydrogen scale (approx. 4.5 eV) and Eg is the band gap value of photocatalyst. The Ⅹ values were 4.73 eV for CN and 5.81 eV for CoFe, as mentioned in literature. The values of Eg for CN and CoFe, as evaluated from UV–vis DRS, were found to be 2.91 eV and 1.39 eV, respectively. Thus, the corresponding ECB and EVB values were estimated to be −1.24 eV and +1.68 eV for CN; and +0.61 eV and +2.00 eV for CoFe using Eqs. (2) and (3). All the calculated values have been enumerated in Table 2.

To examine the separation of photogenerated charge carriers occurring in fabricated heterostructure, PL spectra was recorded. Generally, the higher PL peak intensity demonstrates the fast recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs; on the other hand, lower PL peak intensity defines the higher separation and lower charge recombination (Zhu et al., 2023). PL spectra of pristine CN, CNCoFe-20 and CDs modified CNCoFe-20 heterostructures were recorded at the emission wavelength of 450 nm (given the excitation wavelength of CN = 320 nm) and are illustrated in Fig. 6. (d). PL spectra clearly discloses that pristine CN has exalted emission intensity, revealing the highest recombination of photoinduced charge carriers; whereas it is worth mentioning that upon the formation of heterostructure systems, the PL emission intensity reduced in comparison to pure CN, with 0.5-CDCNCoFe displaying lowest PL intensity unveiling the highest separation of photoinduced charge carriers and consequently leading to its exceptional photocatalytic performance.

The textural properties of fabricated CN, CNCoFe-20 and 0.5-CDCNCoFe were obtained by N2 absorption-desorption isotherms as presented in Fig. S3. These measurements revealed the type-IV isotherms, suggesting the presence of mesopores and macropores in pristine, binary and ternary heterostructures (Garg et al., 2023a). Furthermore, BET surface area for CN, CNCoFe-20 and 0.5-CDCNCoFe was evaluated to be 44.9, 55.6 and 77.0 m2/g, respectively. The results depicted the enhancement in surface area upon the formation of ternary heterostructures, thereby leading to enhancement in photocatalytic performance by 0.5-CDCNCoFe.

The electrochemical performance of all the prepared samples were investigated and characterized using cyclic voltammetry (CV) recorded within the potential range of −1.0 V to + 1.5 V using 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- present alongwith 0.1 M KCl solution as supporting electrolyte. On comparing the CVs of all the synthesized catalysts modified GCE procured at 100 mVs−1 ( Fig. 7. (a)), the redox signals for 0.5-CDCNCoFe modified GCE was perceived to be highest when compared to pristine CN, CoFe, binary CNCoFe-20 and CDs modified ternary heterostructures, attributing to the excellent electrical conductivity provided by 0.5-CDCNCoFe. The variation in peak current (Ip) was observed with the changing scan rate from 10 – 300 mVs−1 for 0.5-CDCNCoFe, as depicted in Fig. S4. The effective surface area of modified electrodes was evaluated using Randles-Sevcik equation given in Eq. (4) (Garg et al., 2023b):
(4)

p
= 2.69 ×

Where Ip is peak current (A), n = number of electrons, A is the area of electrode (cm2), Co is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, DR is diffusion coefficient of the molecules in solution (cm2s−1) and v is scan rate. For 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4-, n = 1 and DR = 7.6 × 10−6 cm2s−1. Based on the obtained peak current values at different scan rate, electroactive surface area of CN, CoFe2O4, CNCoFe-20, 0.25-CDCNCoFe, 0.5-CDCNCoFe, 0.75-CDCNCoFe and 1-CDCNCoFe modified GCE was found to be 0.0177 cm2, 0.0175 cm2, 0.0180 cm2, 0.0185 cm2, 0.0244 cm2, 0.0196 cm2 and 0.0184 cm2, respectively. A linear curve between Ip and v1/2 for 0.5-CDCNCoFe/GCE has been plotted in Fig. 7. (b). The increased active surface area of ternary composite modified GCE in comparison to pure and binary composite modified GCE indicated that the material has propensity to provide more active sites for electron transfer required for the electrochemical reaction to occur.

Further, to introspect the charge transfer properties occurring at the interface of synthesized heterostructures, Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) technique was employed. Typically, the EIS Nyquist plots, given in Fig. 7. (c), encompasses a semicircle arc that determines the charge transfer resistance and the smaller arc radius is indicative of low charge transfer resistance (Sepehrmansourie et al., 2023). Evidencing from these Nyquist plots, CDs modified CNCoFe-20 heterostructures displayed smaller arc radius as compared to pristine CN, CoFe and binary CNCoFe-20 composite. Thus, entailing the higher electron transfer occurring at the interface of CDs modified CNCoFe-20 heterostructures, with 0.5-CDCNCoFe devouring smallest arc radius suggesting the highest charge separation and low interfacial barrier which abetted in heightening of electrochemical sensing potential as well as photocatalytic performance. The corresponding Randles equivalent circuit for 0.5-CDCNCoFe/GCE is presented in Fig. 7. (d), where Rs corresponds to resistance of solution, RCT is charge transfer resistance, Cdl is the capacitance of catalyst/solution interface and W is Warburg impedance. From the circuit, it can be deduced that the introduction of constant phase element described the highly porous nature of fabricated material which assisted in the fast electrocatalytic process and the occurrence of W implies that the process is diffusion controlled.

The photodegradation ability of the fabricated catalysts was investigated through oxidative degradation of SMX under visible light illumination in the presence of chemical oxidant; H2O2. The absorption maxima of SMX appearing at 267 nm in UV–vis spectrophotometer was reconnoitred for measuring the extent of degradation. The reduction in absorption peak intensity of SMX was observed for the prepared catalysts, suggesting the mineralization of SMX molecules.

% degradation was calculated using the following given Eq. (5) (Singh et al., 2022):

Further, SMX degradation by the synthesized catalysts were observed to follow pseudo first order rate kinetics as depicted in Eq. (6) (Kaur et al., 2023a):
(6)

Where k is the first order rate constant. To start with the photocatalytic experiments, optimization of all the reaction conditions is obligatory for best experimental outcomes.

To unveil the role of different reaction parameters for SMX removal, initially, four sets of degradation experiments, involving (i) only light + catalyst, (ii) only catalyst + oxidant, (iii) only light + oxidant and (iv) light + oxidant+ catalyst were performed (where catalyst is 0.5-CDCNCoFe, [catalyst] = 0.5 g/L and [oxidant] = 8.8 mM). % degradation vs time plots for all these experiments are represented in Fig. S5. In the absence of catalyst or light, SMX barely undergo any degradation; however, in the presence of light and catalyst, the degradation efficiency of photocatalyst was enhanced may be due to the efficacious separation of electron-hole pairs via Z-scheme pathway upon light irradiation (Garg et al., 2023a). Nevertheless, in the presence of all the three components in the reaction mixture, the degradation efficiency was observed to be remarkably boosted because of the coupling of photo-Fenton reagent over Z-scheme system, thereby indicating the obligatory presence of light, catalyst and oxidant in the photocatalytic experiments for the effectual removal of SMX (Kaur et al., 2023b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH, catalyst dosage and H2O2 concentration on SMX removal
ce:section.ce:para: Various batch experiments were accomplished to ascertain the impact of reaction parameters over SMX removal by 0.5-CDCNCoFe photocatalyst. Fig. S6. (a) displays the % degradation of SMX at pH values ranging from acidic, neutral to basic (2.5–11.5) with 8.8 mM [H2O2] and 0.5 g/L [catalyst]. The bar graph clearly shows the maximum degradation efficiency at 2.5 pH value, which could be attributed to the maximum oxidation capability of hydroxyl radicals (•OH), formed via photo-Fenton H2O2 mechanism, at pH 2.5 (Singh et al., 2022). Nonetheless, it is noteworthy that the fabricated 0.5CDCNCoFe photocatalyst bestows significant degradation of SMX in the entire pH range, suggesting the versatility of synthesized photo-Fenton scheme together with Z-scheme system towards the elimination of SMX from wastewater. The as-formed catalyst outweighs the disadvantage of traditional photo-Fenton system of working under acidic conditions only.

After ascertaining the appropriate pH value suitable for effective degradation of SMX, the amount of catalyst was optimized by varying the catalyst dosage from 0.25 g/L to 1 g/L keeping pH value to 2.5 and [H2O2] to 8.8 mM, where catalyst employed is 0.5-CDCNCoFe. As presented in Fig. S6. (b), the degradation efficiency was found to enhance with increasing amount from 0.25 g/L to 0.5 g/L due to the enrichment in surface catalytic active sites; however, further increment in catalyst dosage led to reduction in photocatalytic activity attributing to the improper dispersion of catalyst in reaction solution causing turbidity and improper passage for light transmission, thus reducing the light absorption by the designed system (Kaur et al., 2023a). Hence, catalyst dosage of 0.5 g/L was chosen for further photocatalytic experiments.

The concentration of H2O2 greatly influences the photo-Fenton degradation capability of a photocatalyst. Henceforth, the influence of H2O2 concentration from 2.2 mM to 17.6 mM on SMX degradation by 0.5-CDCNCoFe photocatalyst was evaluated and the results are displayed in Fig. S6. (c), where 0.5 g/L of catalyst and pH 2 value was fixed. The removal efficiency enhanced when concentration of H2O2 was augmented from 2.2 mM to 8.8 mM ascribing to the increase in oxidant leading to more formation of •OH radicals which could promote removal of SMX (Singh et al., 2022). Conversely, when H2O2 concentration was increased beyond 8.8 mM, the removal efficiency dropped because excessive H2O2 act as a radical quencher and thus diminishes the production of •OH radicals (Liu et al., 2022). Consequently, 8.8 mM of H2O2 concentration was decided for subsequent studies. Similar kind of results for photo-Fenton degradation reactions have been presented by different researchers in literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative photocatalytic performance evaluation of SMX by synthesized materials
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of all the prepared samples was evaluated by the degradation of SMX under visible light irradiation for 120 min. As can be perceived from Fig. S7. (a), pristine CN and CoFe exhibited 64.7 % and 44.6 % removal of SMX; however, with the formation of binary CNCoFe heterostructure, the % removal boosted to a good extend. With the varying ratios of CoFe in binary heterostructure from 10 % to 40 %, CNCoFe-20 was witnessed to bestow maximum degradation efficiency of 81.5 % in 120 min (Fig. S7.) with highest rate constant value of 0.0149 min−1, suggesting the effectual charge separation via Z-scheme charge transfer occurring in CNCoFe composite (Garg et al., 2022, 2023a).

After the successful combination of CoFe and CN, different weight ratios of CDs from 0.25 wt% to 1 wt% were loaded over the formed CNCoFe-20 heterostructure. Fig. 8. (a) uncovers the % degradation vs time plots by individual, binary and CDs modified composites. As can be discerned from this curve, when compared to single and binary components, CDs modified composites presented higher removal efficiencies of SMX with 0.5-CDCNCoFe rendering significantly higher degradation efficacy of 97.8 %. This enhancement in degradation capability could be ascribed to the additional up-conversion photoluminescence properties of CDs leading to the increment in photons absorption by the synthesized catalyst (Zhang et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2023). These results well matched with the UV–vis DRS and PL outcomes. Table 3 portrays the % degradation of SMX employing different photocatalysts. Undeniably, the increase in CDs loading on CNCoFe-20 from 0 wt% to 0.5 wt% preceded the enhancement in degradation efficacy of heterostructure. Nevertheless, the further proliferation in CDs loading beyond 0.5 wt% resulted in suppression of photocatalytic degradation performance, which could be due to the competition of CDs and CNCoFe-20 for absorption of photons and therefore causing optical shielding effect in the fabricated CDCNCoFe composites. Similar results have been mentioned by Li et al (Li et al., 2021).

The rate kinetic plots (ln (At/Ao) v/s time graphs) for degradation of SMX by all the fabricated materials and their corresponding rate constant values are summarized in Fig. 8. (b) and Table 3, respectively. The increment in the rate kinetic values of CDs modified composites than single and binary components unveiled the augmentation in photocatalytic performance by the integration of CDs in CNCoFe-20 heterostructure. The degradation rate constant values of 0.5-CDCNCoFe were found to be 3.3, 7.3, 2.0, 1.7, 1.3 and 1.7 times higher than CN, CoFe, CNCoFe-20, 0.25-CDCNCoFe, 0.75-CDCNCoFe and 1-CDCNCoFe, respectively, which were in concordance to % removal. The results alluded that introduction of CDs to CNCoFe-20 heterostructure exaggerated the photocatalytic performance via enhancing the light absorption by Z-scheme photocatalyst.

Moreover, the comparative analysis of the photocatalytic performance of prepared material with other photocatalysts reported in literature towards degradation of SMX, as delineated from Table 4, clearly established the robustness of fabricated material.

One of the crucial factor in determining the viability of photocatalyst is to access the mineralization of SMX occurring during photocatalytic degradation reaction (Dhiman et al., 2021). The total organic carbon (TOC) removal rate of SMX by all the fabricated photocatalysts after 120 min of light illumination are displayed in Fig. 9. It can be clearly deciphered from the graph that only 42.1 % and 35.2 % of TOC was eliminated by pristine CN and CoFe, respectively. The introduction of CoFe to CN system boosted the TOC removal rate to around 60 %. Besides, CDs modified composites displayed outstanding TOC removal rate of SMX to around 75 %, 81 %, 78 % and 70 % by 0.25-CDCNCoFe, 0.5-CDCNCoFe, 0.75-CDCNCoFe and 1-CDCNCoFe, respectively. Therefore, TOC measurements endorse the excellent catalytic performance of fabricated 0.5-CDCNCoFe heterostructure in mineralization of SMX from aquatic environment.

For expounding the main radical species involved in the oxidation reaction of SMX, radical scavenging studies were performed. Fig. S8. illustrates the impact on degradation % of SMX by 0.5-CDCNCoFe in the presence of different radical scavengers; where i-propanol (i-PA), ammonium oxalate (AO) and p-benzoquinone (p-BQ) were utilized for scavenging •OH, h+ and •O2 - radicals, respectively (Garg et al., 2022; Ling et al., 2020; Kumar et al., 2023). The reduction in degradation efficiency in the existence of all the three quenchers established the explicit role of all the three corresponding radicals in degradation reaction. The obvious decline in % removal from 97.5 % to 30.3 % upon the addition of i-PA to reaction mixture indicated the remarkable role of •OH radicals in degradation process. Moreover, degradation efficiency dropped to 70 % and 62.3 % after the introduction of AO and p-BQ to the reaction mixture, respectively, revealing the irrefutable role of h+ and •O2 - radicals in the removal process of SMX. From the radical trapping experiments, the requisite role of all the three •OH, h+ and •O2 - radicals in degradation process was confirmed, with •OH radicals playing the key role.

In addition, to confirm the aforementioned findings, several scavengers were used in the trials to capture free radicals. Methanol and tert-Butanol were used as hydroxyl radical scavengers, whereas triethanolamine and KI were chosen as hole-trapping agents. Chloroform and 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinyloxy (TEMPL) were used to study superoxide radicals. According to Fig. S8., the photocatalytic efficiency of 0.5-CDCNCoFe for TCs reduced dramatically when all the quenchers were present. This highlights the important involvement of the four reactive species in the degradation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of various coexisting inorganic and organic species
ce:section.ce:para: The ubiquitous presence of inorganic anions and natural organic matter in natural water matrix generally impacts the analytical performance of as-synthesized materials towards degradation of SMX. Thus, degradation profile of SMX by 0.5-CDCNCoFe photocatalyst in the existence of 5 mM of various inorganic anions (Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, HCO3 - and HPO4 -) was analyzed and presented in Fig. 10. (a). The degradation efficacy underwent only a slight disturbance with Cl-, SO4 2- and NO3 -, while HCO3 - and HPO4 - adversely impact the degradation performance markedly in the case of HPO4 -. The inhibitory effect of later two anions could be attributed to the fact that HCO3 - and HPO4 - radicals increase in pH of reaction mixture which does not foster the formation of •OH radicals as already observed from the pH studies mentioned earlier (Fig. S6. (a)), the increasing pH led to the reduction in elimination efficiency. Moreover, HCO3 - and HPO4 - act as radical quencher for •OH and h+, thus resulting in lesser number of free radicals available for degradation reaction (Deng et al., 2022).

Humic acid (HA), the major constituent of natural organic matter, generally coexist in wastewater alongwith SMX; therefore, to examine the impact of HA on photocatalytic degradation of SMX by 0.5-CDCNCoFe heterostructure, varying concentrations of HA from 5, 10, 15 and 20 mg/L (Fig. 10. (b)). It is noteworthy that with the increasing concentration of HA, the overall degradation % exhibited a significant drop from 97.5 % to 62.7 %, which could be attributed to the two substantial factors. Firstly, the larger molecules of HA may impede the light penetration into the reaction mixture; thereby inhibiting the visible light absorption by photocatalyst. Secondly, HA itself consume •OH radicals produced during the reaction making less availability of radicals for degradation reaction as a result of which the removal rate of SMX deteriorated (Li et al., 2023b). Thus, these studies provide a cogent evidence about the impact of different coexisting inorganic and organic species on the photocatalytic activity of fabricated material in wastewater treatment.

To explore the mechanism involved during the degradation of SMX by CDCNCoFe heterostructure, the band structure was elucidated on the basis of conduction band (ECB) and valence band (EVB) edge potentials. When the fabricated CDs modified heterostructure was illuminated with visible light irradiation, the electron-hole pairs start forming with assembling of electrons and holes in CB and VB, respectively (Eqs. (7) and (8)). Upon the formation of heterojunction, photogenerated charge carriers’ migration mode can occur via two different routes; namely Type-II and Z-scheme (Dhiman et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021b). Following Type-II charge carriers’ movement, the photogenerated electrons in CB of CN would jump to CB to CoFe, while photoinduced holes in VB of CoFe would migrate to VB of CN. However, the accumulated electrons in CB of CoFe did not possess enough potential (ECB = +0.61 eV) to reduce O2 to •O2 - radicals (redox potential of O2/•O2 - is −0.33 eV) (Garg et al., 2022). Likewise, amassed holes congregated in VB of CN (EVB = +1.68 eV) were incapable to oxidize OH- to •OH radicals (redox potential of HO•/OH- is +1.99 eV) (Garg et al., 2023a). Absurdly, from the radical scavenging studies, the main reactive species responsible for degradation of SMX were perceived to be •OH, h+ and •O2 -. Enlightening from these results, Z-scheme pathway provides a more reasonable degradation route as illustrated in Fig. 11. Herein, the electrons residing in the CB of CoFe migrated to VB of CN and recombined with holes. Moreover, this transference of electrons was further assisted via CDs that could act as electron mediator due to their excellent charge transfer properties (Wu et al., 2023). Consequently, photoinduced electrons and holes get accrued in CN and CoFe, respectively, thereby improving the charge separation capability. Now, the electrons prevailing in the CB of CN was negative enough (ECB = −1.24 eV) to effectively reduce O2 to •O2 - radicals, correspondingly, the holes existing in the VB of CoFe was positive enough (EVB = +2.0 eV) to oxidize OH- to •OH radicals, as given in Eqs. (9) and (10). Thus, the Z-scheme charge transfer pathway could commendably preserve the redox properties of synthesized heterostructure to facilitate the formation of free radical species. Additionally, the CDs present in the fabricated material converted the long wavelength infrared light as well as portion of visible light to corresponding short-wavelength radiations owing to the up-converted PL properties of CDs which amplified the light absorption by CDs modified CNCoFe (Li et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2023).

Afterwards, when H2O2 was added to the reaction mixture, photo-Fenton mechanism also get instigated (Zhang et al., 2020). The photoinduced electrons in the CB of CN triggered the reduction of Fe(III) to Fe(II) that would react with added H2O2 to yield •OH radicals, which were the main reactive species involved in degradation reaction. The overall photo-Fenton reactions are presented by Eqs. (11) and (12). Furthermore, H2O2 could capture the photoexcited electrons present in CB of CN leading to the generation of •OH, thus promoting the electron-hole pair separation which in turn enhanced the photocatalytic performance (Eq. 13). The as-generated radicals assisted the efficacious degradation of organic pollutants as given in equation 14. Benefitting from the synergistic effect of up-conversion properties of CDs, photo-Fenton process and Z-scheme charge transfer mechanism, CDs modified CNCoFe heterostructures exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance towards SMX degradation (Wang et al., 2021b; Hu et al., 2023; Lei et al., 2021).

The recyclability, stability and reusability of the synthesized Z-scheme photocatalyst are the key parameters that defines the practical applicability of materials (Kaur et al., 2023b). To explore the same, impact on degradation efficiency of 0.5-CDCNCoFe catalyst was explored upto six experimental runs conducted under identical conditions. After each degradation run, the catalyst was separated using external magnet followed by its washing with water and ethanol and subsequent drying at 80 ᵒC for 10 h. The recovered catalyst was quantified and the recovery % was observed to be more than 95 %, deciphering the higher efficiency with minimal loss of the catalyst. The degradation results of six consecutive experiments are portrayed in Fig. S9. (a). The % removal of SMX, as observed from the bar graph, was almost more than 90 % that insinuates the excellent reusability of as-prepared heterostructure. Besides, no evident change in the XRD pattern of 0.5-CDCNCoFe (Fig. S9. (b)) connotes that the crystalline structure of photocatalyst remain intact even after photocatalytic degradation experiments, corroborating the stability of designed photocatalyst. The study demonstrates that the fabricated heterostructure can prove to be a propitious candidate in wastewater treatment applications.

Different real water matrix greatly influences the photocatalytic performance of the synthesized catalyst. Considering the realistic application of photocatalyst, various real water samples were collected and utilized as reaction matrix for photocatalytic degradation of SMX by 0.5-CDCNCoFe. For this exploration, double distilled water, tap water, lake water (obtained from Sukhna lake, Chandigarh, India) and river water (procured from Ganga river, Haridwar, India) were filtered followed by spiking with SMX; the whole analytical photocatalytic degradation procedure conditions being alike. As shown in Fig. S10., the removal % of SMX in double distilled water, tap water, lake water and river water were 96.5 %, 95.4 %, 92.2 % and 86.7 %, respectively. The degradation efficiency was observed to be maximum when real matrix was double distilled water; however, the removal efficiency was reduced in lake water and river water matrix attributing to the simultaneous presence of different competing inorganic/organic species in the matrix. Nevertheless, the degradation performance of designed 0.5-CDCNCoFe heterostructure in the removal of SMX from real water samples was good enough to make it a potent material for realistic wastewater detoxification (Garg et al., 2022; Kaur et al., 2023a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical sensing of SMX and TMP
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the inimitable opto-electronic properties exhibited by nanostructured materials, they have been extensively employed for the rapid, selective and sensitive determination of antibiotics in wastewater. On the similar grounds, the fabricated 0.5-CDCNCoFe catalyst was employed for the simultaneous detection of SMX and TMP. Before pursuing towards electrochemical detection, firstly SWV curves of bare GCE and 0.5-CDCNCoFe/GCE were recorded in the presence of 0.25 µmolL−1 concentration of each SMX and TMP in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). As can be observed from Fig. S11., the current response (Ipo ’ for SMX and Ipo ” for TMP) for 0.5-CDCNCoFe/GCE was exceptionally higher than bare GCE which could be accredited to the facilitative electron transfer ability and high charge transfer kinetics proffered by modified electrode that resulted in high electrocatalytic activity towards simultaneous determination of SMX and TMP. Henceforth, the forthcoming studies employ the utilization of 0.5-CDCNCoFe/GCE for electrochemical sensing of SMX and TMP.

To explore the analytical performance of fabricated sensor towards the detection of SMX and TMP, firstly different electrochemical techniques, namely linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), CV and SWV were compared for the simultaneous detection of analytes. The results are demonstrated in Fig. S12. (a) which clearly showed that the current response was maximum in case of SWV, hence SWV technique was engaged for the further detection studies of analytes.

Afterwards, to procure the best experimental conditions for electrochemical detection, various SWV parameters such as deposition potential and deposition time were optimized. Fig. S12. (b) represents the variation in current response on varying the deposition potential from −0.8 V to −1.8 V in the presence of 0.5 µM of each analyte in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). The peak currents of SMX and TMP were observed to be augmented with increasing deposition potential from −0.8 V to −1.5 V, with reduction in current value later. Besides, Fig. S12. (c) depicts the change in current response with altering deposition time from 5 sec to 20 sec and the maximum peak current for both SMX and TMP was obtainable after 10 sec of deposition time. Therefore, deposition potential of −1.5 V and deposition time of 10 sec was chosen for subsequent electrochemical detection of SMX and TMP by 0.5-CDCNCoFe modified GCE using SWV technique.

pH of the electrolyte greatly impacts the electrochemical current response of sensor owing to its influence on electrochemical reaction mechanism (Kaur et al., 2021). Therefore, the effect of pH on the peak current (Ip) and peak potential (Ep) values was investigated by recording SWV curves of 0.25 µmolL−1 concentration of each SMX and TMP in 0.1 M PBS of different pH value ranging from 5 to 10. As displayed in Fig. S13. (a) and Fig. S13. (b), with increasing pH value from 5 to 7, Ep value shifts towards higher oxidation potentials with the corresponding increment in Ip and reached its maxima at pH 7. However, further increase in pH beyond 7 leads to the decline in Ip. Undeniably, the increase in Ipo ” of TMP was higher in comparison to Ipo ’ of SMX for the same concentration. For the successive sensing experiments of SMX and TMP, pH 7 was chosen as the optimal value.

Additionally, the graph demonstrating Ep v/s pH (Fig. S13. (c)) shows good linear relationship fitting to the equation given as Ep ’(V) = −0.0523 pH + 1.2705 and Ep ” = −0.0334 pH +1.3787 for SMX and TMP, respectively. In accordance to Nernst equation, the slope for SMX is 52.3 mV which is in close proximity to Nernstian value (59 mV) which implies the involvement of same number of electrons and protons in electro-oxidation of SMX; whereas slope of TMP is 33.4 mV indicating the exchange of one proton and two electrons during electro-oxidation of TMP (Javar et al., 2022; Cesarino et al., 2013).

The effect of scan rate on the electrochemical reactions of SMX and TMP was also examined by recording CV of 0.1 M PBS buffer containing 0.25 µmolL−1 each SMX and TMP over different scan rate ranging from 10 mVs−1 to 250 mVs−1. Ip v/s scan rate (v) and Ip v/s v1/2 were plotted (Fig. S14. (a) and Fig. S14. (b)) to analyse the electrode reaction kinetics that displayed a good linearity with R2 > 0.98, indicating that the process occurring at the electrode surface is both adsorption and diffusion controlled. Although, the linearity is observed for both Ip v/s v and Ip v/s v1/2, the lower slope values of Ip v/s v1/2 for both SMX and TMP specified the diffusion process to be more prominent over adsorption process. Same was further confirmed by plotting log Ip v/s log v (Fig. S14. (c)) which exhibited the slope value close to 0.6 for both the analytes, thereby endorsing the diffusion controlled electrochemical reaction (Guaraldo et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Individual detection of SMX and TMP
ce:section.ce:para: Using optimized conditions for SWV, 0.5-CDCNCoFe modified GCE was employed to investigate its electrochemical behaviour as a function of SMX and TMP concentrations. The oxidation peak potentials of SMX and TMP were discerned around +0.9 V and +1.2 V, respectively. As can be seen in Fig. 12. (a), with the gradual increase in concentration of SMX from 0.05 µM to 1 µM, the current response also increases (keeping the concentration of TMP constant to 0.1 µM) in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). The analytic response in the range of 0.05 µM to 1 µM of SMX portrayed linearity described by the equation Ipo ’ (µA) = 17.773 [SMX] (µM) + 0.2663 (R2 ∼ 0.99) (Fig. 12. (b)). Similarly, as shown in Fig. 12. (c) and Fig. 12. (d), upon increasing the concentration of TMP from 0.05 µM to 1 µM in 0.1 PBS (pH 7), keeping the concentration of SMX constant to 0.35 µM, the current response increased linearly obeying the equation given as Ipo ” (µA) = 23.104 [TMP] (µM) + 3.0796 (R2 ∼ 0.99). According to IUPAC recommendations, limit of detection (LOD) can be evaluated using 3σ/m, where σ is the standard deviation and m is the slope of calibration curve (Garg et al., 2023b). For the individual detection of SMX and TMP, LOD was calculated to be 0.041 µM and 0.048 µM for SMX and TMP, respectively. These ramifications indicate that the fabricated sensor can serve as a potential candidate to discretely detect SMX and TMP without interfering or impacting the sensing analyses of each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of SMX and TMP
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the electrochemical performance of proposed sensor towards concurrent detection of SMX and TMP, electrochemical response of 0.5-CDCNCoFe/GCE was recorded by simultaneously varying the concentration of SMX and TMP from 0.05 µM to 1 µM in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). It is evident from Fig. 13. (a) and Fig. 13. (b), SWV current response increases uniformly with synchronized intensification of concentration of both the analytes. The linear response of SMX was expressed by the equation Ipo ’ (µA) = 17.393 [SMX] (µM) + 0.5306 (R2 ∼ 0.99) and TMP by Ipo ” (µA) = 22.489 [TMP] (µM) + 3.2748 (R2 ∼ 0.99). The corresponding detection limits of SMX and TMP were 0.042 µM and 0.047 µM. The results of simultaneous detection matched well with the independent detection of analytes entailing that the prepared sensor divulged admirable performance towards detection of SMX and TMP individually without any interfering with current response of each other.

A comparison of different electrochemical sensors employed for the individual and simultaneous detection of SMX and TMP in literature are tabulated in Table 5. It can be inferred from the table that the proposed sensor displayed significant lower detection limit as compared to other sensors for both the individual as well as simultaneous electrochemical sensing of SMX and TMP, hence the prepared heterostructure may have promising application in detection of SMX and TMP in aqueous samples and may be used as alternate material in environmental analysis.

The electrochemical behaviour of sensor to detect the analytes in the presence of different interferents in real world limits the performance of designed sensor (Kaur et al., 2021). To examine the validity of proposed sensor in practical world, its electrochemical response was measured for 0.25 µmolL−1 concentration of each SMX and TMP along with the presence of 10-fold excess of potential interferents including inorganic ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, SO4 2-, NO3 +) and organic species (glucose, ascorbic acid and urea). As observed from Fig. S15., the current response of sensor for SMX and TMP did not undergo any major alteration even with the occurrence of excess amount of interferents, confirming the high selectivity of proposed sensor to detect SMX and TMP in wastewater.

Repeatability, reproducibility and stability are the key governing factors that determines performance of the proposed sensor. Therefore, the repeatability measurements were done by recording the current response of same modified electrode for six replicating cycles in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7) containing 0.25 µmolL−1 concentration of each SMX and TMP (Fig. S16. (a)). No evident change in current response was perceived with relative standard deviation (RSD) values of around 3.6 % and 2.3 % for SMX and TMP, respectively, which substantiates the acceptable repeatability of fabricated sensor. Afterwards, to introspect the reproducibility of proposed sensor, the current response of five different electrodes modified by 0.5-CDCNCoFe was deliberated in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7) consisting of 0.25 µmolL−1 concentration of each SMX and TMP. As can be witnessed from Fig. S16. (b), similar current response with RSD value less than 1 % was attainable for all the electrodes, signifying the excellent reproducibility of the proposed sensor. Further, to investigate the stability of 0.5-CDCNCoFe/GCE, the current response in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH 7) containing 0.25 µmolL−1 concentration of each SMX and TMP for the same modified electrode was recorded repeatedly over 30 days for 20 runs. Fig. S16. (c) indicates that around 93 % and 94 % current response of initial values for SMX and TMP was still retained by the electrode stored in refrigerator at 4 °C, specifying the high stability of proposed sensor.

To endorse the analytical performance of proposed sensor in real world, simultaneous electrochemical detection of SMX and TMP was done by employing standard addition method, in which different known concentrations of both the analytes were spiked in assorted water samples, namely tap water and lake water. The spiking amounts and their respective recovery % values and RSD has been mentioned in Table 6. The recoveries of SMX and TMP were quite satisfactory with % values ranging between 96 % and 105 % having % RSD less than 4 %. These results established the reliability and feasibility of proposed method for simultaneous detection of SMX and TMP with good precision and accuracy.

Aiming at the environmental decontamination, herein, a series of novel Tulsi leaves (Ocimum Sanctum) derived CDs modified Z-scheme CN/CoFe heterojunction system (0.25-CDCNCoFe, 0.5-CDCNCoFe, 0.75-CDCNCoFe and 1-CDCNCoFe) were concocted via simple and pecuniary sonication-calcination route. The structural and morphological characteristics were confirmed by XRD, FT-IR, XPS, FE-SEM and HR-TEM analysis. The photocatalytic performance of CDCNCoFe for SMX was pragmatic to be augmented in comparison to individual CN and CoFe and binary CNCoFe with degradation rate kinetics of 0.5-CDCNCoFe be 3.3, 7.1 and 2.0 times higher than pristine CN, CoFe and CNCoFe-20, respectively. This was ascribed to formation of Z-scheme heterostructure which assisted in enhanced separation of charge carriers with lesser recombination, as unveiled by PL and EIS studies. Also, the heightening in visible light absorption due to up-conversion property exhibited by CDs in CDCNCoFe system could precede the furthering of photocatalytic activity. Photo-Fenton assisted Z-scheme mechanistic pathway involving oxidation of H2O2 was witnessed to be accountable for degradation of SMX. Radical quenching studies described the inevitable role of •OH, h+ and •O2 - radicals in degradation process. Moreover, HCO3 -, HPO4 - and humic acid asserted negative impact on degradation performance of synthesized material displayed. Furthermore, owing to excellent electrocatalytic response of 0.5-CDCNCoFe, it was explored for selective detection of SMX and TMP using SWV. The proposed sensor presented excellent method for individual as well as simultaneous detection of SMX and TMP with corresponding LODs of 0.042 µM and 0.047 µM in the linear range of 0.05 µM to 1 µM in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7). The dynamism of proposed system was substantiated by removal and detection of SMX in real water samples. Concluding, this methodology endows a new platform for devising multi-functional nanostructures for practical applications in wastewater treatment.
